title: Module 3 quiz: Tool use and agents

questions.prompt: A tool definition has three required parts. What are they?

choices.text: Name (snake_case identifier), description (what the tool does — write like API docs), and input_schema (JSON Schema for arguments). Description quality drives selection quality; vague descriptions cause Claude to pick the wrong tool.

questions.explanation: Anthropic's tool format is name + description + input_schema. The description is the model's only signal for when to call the tool, so write it specifically and completely. Vague tool descriptions are the most common cause of wrong-tool selection.

questions.prompt: An agent is shipped without `maxSteps`. A user asks an ambiguous question; the agent loops fetching the same tool repeatedly until the next morning. What's the production-ready fix?

choices.text: Cap iterations with `maxSteps` (10–20 default), alert on consecutive tool errors, add per-tool timeouts, and treat hitting the cap as an explicit error: escalate to human or return partial answer — never silently retry.
Trust the model not to loop.

questions.explanation: Hard caps are non-negotiable in agent design. maxSteps prevents unbounded loops; timeouts prevent hung tools; alerts surface repeated errors before they become a bill. Plus approval gates on irreversible tools.

questions.prompt: When Claude returns multiple tool_use blocks in one response, what should you do?

choices.text: Execute them serially in the order returned.

Execute them in parallel with Promise.all — Claude only returns multiple tool_use blocks when calls are independent. Match each tool_result back by tool_use_id, not position. Often a 3–4× speedup on multi-tool steps.

questions.explanation: Claude is smart about parallelization — multiple tool_use blocks in one response means the calls don't depend on each other. Execute in parallel; match results by tool_use_id. The serial-vs-parallel decision is in your runtime, not the model.

questions.prompt: You need strict typed JSON output from Claude. What's the cleanest pattern?

choices.text: Define a tool whose input_schema matches the output. Force it via `tool_choice: { type: 'tool', name: '...' }`. Use enums and required fields aggressively. Read typed arguments from the tool_use block — no parsing, no failures.

questions.explanation: Tool-call structured output is the canonical Claude pattern for typed data. Schema is enforced, optional/required fields are first-class, nested structures are reliable. Cleaner than prefilling for anything with real schema complexity.

choices.text: An open standard for exposing tools, resources, and prompts to LLMs uniformly. Build an integration once as an MCP server; any MCP-aware client uses it. Eliminates the 'every app reimplements every integration' pattern.
A way to encrypt API calls.

questions.explanation: MCP is the deployment layer on top of tool calling. It standardizes integrations so they're reusable across Claude Desktop, IDE clients, and custom apps. Use inline tools for one-off needs; promote to MCP when the integration is reused or shared.
